query: association between endometrial polyps and overexpression of VEGF and TGF-beta1

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Polyp -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial polyps are localized overgrowths of the endometrial glands and stroma that protrude out of the endometrium. The definite etiology of endometrial polyps is unknown. However, endometrial polyps are associated with endometrial hyperplasia; therefore, unopposed estrogen is considered to be a risk factor. [1] Other mechanisms that have been associated with the development of polyps include increased endometrial aromatase activity, TGF-beta, VEGF, BCL-2, and genetic factors. [2] [3] [4] [5] Endometrial abnormalities, including the development of polyps, are associated with chronic tamoxifen therapy and occur in 20% to 35% of females. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hormonal therapy resistance is seen in nearly 30% of patients. This is thought to be due to a decreased availability of progesterone receptors and alteration of the apoptotic signaling pathway of the endometrial glandular cells. [21] [15] Additional factors that may be responsible include the down-regulation of progesterone receptor and activation of the transforming growth factor signaling pathway. Less likely, resistance to hormonal therapy could result from mutations in PR or possibly paracrine effects. The histologic response of the glands of AEH/EIN is strongly coupled to the decidual response in the stroma, so the possibility of a paracrine effect is plausible, but the epithelial-stromal interactions of the endometrium are incompletely understood. [21] [15] [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Adrenocortical Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) or Gardner syndrome, which is a mutation of the adenomatous polyposis coli gene (APC) located in 5q21 chromosomal, present with multiple colonic polyps and an increased risk of early colon carcinomas. Furthermore, FAP is associated with pigmented retinal lesions, desmoids tumors, osteomas, thyroid adenoma/carcinomas, and other different malignant tumors. Adrenocortical tumors, especially nonfunctional nodular hyperplasia, cortisol-producing adenomas (CPA), and ACC occur in 7 to 13% of patients with FAP. FAP results from a germline inactivating mutation of APC, a tumor suppressor gene that inhibits Wnt/beta-catenin signaling. According to Knudson’s model, ACC in patients with FAP exhibits somatic APC mutations as a second hit. The activation of the Wnt/b-catenin signaling pathway occurs in a third of ACC cases, and less commonly observed in adrenocortical adenoma. Activation of b-catenin is mainly related to the mutation of the Catenin beta-1 gene (CTNNB1). Consistently, transcriptome studies have shown an overexpression of Wnt/b-catenin target genes in ACC. [10] Mutations of CTNNB1 or a histologic pattern of its activation are associated with poor outcomes. [1] [11] Somatic mutations in APC has a prevalence of 2 to 3%, suggesting the importance of somatic alterations of other mechanisms in the Wnt/beta-catenin pathway in causing ACC. Indeed, activating somatic mutations of beta-catenin itself have been reported with a prevalence of about 16% in large cohorts of ACC. [12] Interestingly, p53 and beta-catenin mutations are frequent in ACC with poor prognosis but are almost mutually exclusive. Constitutive activation of beta-catenin in the adrenal cortex of transgenic mice resulted in progressive steroidogenic and undifferentiated spindle-shaped cell hyperplasia, which lead to macroscopic adenomas development. [12] Malignant characteristics such as uncontrolled neovascularization and loco-regional metastatic invasion appeared only later in this context, suggesting the necessity of other associated genetic alterations. More recently, researchers identified a new actor with the Wnt/beta-catenin pathway involved in adrenal tumorigenesis. Zinc and ring finger 3 (ZNRF3), a cell-surface transmembrane E3 ubiquitin ligase that is a negative regulator of the Wnt/b-catenin pathway. ZNRF3 leads the Wingless-related integration site-low density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 6 (Wnt-LRP6) receptor complex to degradation. ZNRF3 is regulated by the R-Spondin protein that regulates the association of ZNRF3 with the related leucine-rich repeat-containing G protein-coupled receptors LGR4. This association results in membrane clearance of ZNFR3 and activation of the Wnt/b-catenin pathway. Recently, ZNRF3 was found to be the most frequently altered gene in 2 large cohorts of ACC, with a prevalence of respectively 21% and 19%. [1] [13] The transcriptome of tumors with alterations of ZNFR3 shows activation of b-catenin targets but milder than the level observed in CTNNB1-mutated ACC. ZNFR3 also constitutes a new tumor suppressor gene. By the sum of the CTNNB1- and ZNRF3-altered ACC, activation of the Wnt/b-catenin pathway could be present in 39% of ACC. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Receptivity -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Implantation failure can be caused by 1) issues related to embryo development and/or aneuploidy, 2) reduced or absent endometrial receptivity, and 3) embryo-endometrial asynchrony. [1] Factors that disturb receptivity include inflammatory events, thin endometria, fibroids, polyps, septa, immunologically mediated disturbances, and endocrine causes. [18] Endometrial receptivity is not an all-or-nothing phenomenon but rather exists on a spectrum. A mild defect in receptivity may cause placental abnormalities, which lead to issues like pre-eclampsia or low birth weight. More severe forms of receptivity aberrations may lead to early pregnancy loss, and in complete loss of receptivity, infertility may rise. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Polyp -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial polyps can have a negative impact on the quality of life and mental health of patients who have a poor understanding of the disease. Patients should be counseled on the disease pathology keeping in mind that terms like “lesion” or “neoplasm” may cause excessive concern for the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Receptivity -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Estrogen dominance results in increased cell proliferation, inflammation, and angiogenesis, exacerbating the non-receptive endometrial state. In studies of endometriosis, a condition defined by excess estrogen and progesterone resistance, it has been shown that the resulting inflammation of the endometrium is associated with an overexpression of p450 aromatase, which changes the dynamic of progesterone-to-estrogen activity. The overexpression of aromatase has been associated with poor IVF outcomes. [24] Endometrial receptivity continues to be a very active area of research. The contributing factors to endometrial receptivity have not all been recognized and continue to be investigated.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: TP53 inactivation as a major driver of most serous carcinomas (type 2), some high-grade endometrioid carcinomas (type 1), and many uterine carcinosarcomas; and inactivation of either gene as drivers of some clear cell carcinomas. [5] [6] Mutation or inactivation of TP53 is an early event in the pathogenesis of serous tumors and is seen in nearly 75% of EICs, which precede serous carcinomas. [2] Other molecular events implicated in the pathogenesis of serous carcinoma include somatic mutations in PPP2R1A , FBXW7, SPOP, CHD4, and TAF1; amplification and/or overexpression of ERBB2, MYC, and CCNE1 (cyclin-E); and overexpression of p16 and synuclein-γ. [2] [5] The altered PI3K pathway is also detected in serous carcinomas, mainly due to mutations in PIK3CA (in 17–43% of tumors), with some showing PTEN and PIK3R1 mutation. [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Enhancing our understanding of the premalignant stages of EC development will allow us to pursue earlier diagnosis and facilitate appropriate stratification of women at risk of developing EC, permitting timely and appropriate therapeutic interventions. This involves an interprofessional team approach. Patients will often present to their primary care provider with abnormal vaginal bleeding. These physicians, physician assistants, and nurse practitioners in obstetrics/gynecology and family medicine will be the first-line providers to assess the patient. To assist with the diagnosis, these providers will rely on pathologists to review and diagnose endometrial tissue samples.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Polyp -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial polyps refer to overgrowths of endometrial glands and stroma within the uterine cavity. Endometrial polyps vary in size from a few millimeters to several centimeters in diameter. Polyps may be found as a single lesion or multiple lesions filling the entire endometrial cavity. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of endometrial polyps and explains the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Longstanding molecular observations implicate PTEN inactivation, and it is seen in over 70% of the lesions. Thus, PTEN appears to be an early driver of atypical endometrial hyperplasia. [7] The systematic sequencing of endometrioid carcinomas has identified over 200 protein-encoding genes as pathogenic driver genes for type 1 EC. [3] Overall, there is dysregulation of the PI3K–PTEN–AKT–mTOR, RAS–MEK–ERK, and WNT–β-catenin pathways; microsatellite instability (MSI), mismatch repair (MMR) defects; and a relatively high occurrence of POLE mutations associated with a rare ultra-mutated phenotype. [5] The mutation of the ARID1A tumor suppressor gene may also be seen. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Summarize the appropriate evaluation of premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Outline the management options available for premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Identify strategies for improving care coordination and communication among the interprofessional team to improve outcomes for premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Cancer -- Pathophysiology -- Pathophysiological Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: Immunologic mechanisms also contribute to malignant endometrial transformation. While the immune system is normally protective against pathogenic changes, study results have identified immunologic factors that promote progression from EIN to endometrial cancer, including tumor-associated macrophages, fibroblasts, and myofibroblasts. In contrast to benign endometrial tissue, endometrial cancers exhibit elevated tumor-associated macrophage density within the endometrial epithelium and stroma. This heightened density correlates with increased myometrial and vascular invasion occurrences, lymph node metastasis, and heightened expression levels of Ki-67 and p53. Additionally, endometrial cancers often present with higher stage and grade compared to benign counterparts. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Receptivity -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: First and foremost, when an endometrial receptivity defect is due to an endometrial polyp or submucosal myoma, there is good evidence that removing these structures improves implantation and pregnancy outcomes. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: AUB etiologies differ based on hormonal and structural influences, though the exact pathophysiologic mechanisms may be unclear. For instance, ovulatory dysfunction resulting in disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis arises from unpredictable progesterone withdrawal, leading to unregulated endometrial growth and shedding. In contrast, endometrial dysfunction is linked to impaired vascular and hemostatic responses, often involving diminished hypoxia signaling and abnormal endometrial repair. [3] Structural causes like fibroids and adenomyosis further complicate the picture, disrupting myometrial contractility, paracrine signaling, and vascular function. For example, fibroids may induce excessive bleeding through mechanisms, eg, increased endometrial surface area, altered angiogenesis, and impaired clotting factor expression. The precise mechanisms linking polyps to AUB have not been identified. [3] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone, a synthetic progestogenic compound, may be used to treat انتباذ بطاني رحمي (endometrial hyperplasia). By supplying progestin activity, 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone counteracts estrogen‑driven proliferation of the uterine lining, helping to normalize endometrial thickness and lower the risk of progression to malignancy. This therapeutic application highlights the role of hormonal modulation in managing intrauterine disorders and underscores the importance of appropriate hormonal therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are interconnected medical conditions. Prevention and control measures are applicable to both female infertility and endometrial implantation, and the two conditions are related, indicating that endometrial implantation can contribute to or be associated with female infertility and that managing endometrial pathology is important in the care of women with infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine/endometrial disorders are classified as a subset within the broader category of enlarged uterine leiomyoma tumors (أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة), indicating that conditions affecting the uterus and endometrium are encompassed by the pathology of large fibroid growths. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing fibroid enlargement as a central component in the spectrum of uterine/endometrial disease and suggests that assessment of fibroid size and impact is a key aspect of managing patients with uterine/endometrial disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is closely related to endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). Abnormalities in the process of endometrial implantation can impair the uterine lining’s ability to support embryo attachment, thereby contributing to infertility in women. Recognizing the connection between these two conditions is important for clinicians when evaluating reproductive health and planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث), endometrial adhesions (انتباذ بطاني رحمي), and mitral valve stenosis (تضيُّق الصمام المترالي) are all recognized diagnoses. Endometrial adhesions are related to female infertility, indicating that intrauterine scarring can be a contributing factor to infertility in women. Mitral valve stenosis represents a separate cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the mitral valve. Understanding the link between endometrial adhesions and female infertility, alongside the distinct nature of mitral valve stenosis, is important for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 670
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone are hormonal agents that may help prevent spontaneous abortion (إجهاض تلقائي). Clinical observations indicate that a history of congenital malformation in a previous pregnancy (CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY) is associated both with an increased risk of spontaneous abortion and with the presence of a uterine smooth muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس رحلي). Similarly, the parameter “SIZE DATES” and an unknown type of uterine scar (UTERINE SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN) are each clinically linked to spontaneous abortion as well as to uterine smooth muscle tumors, suggesting that gestational size assessments and uterine scar status may serve as markers for both miscarriage risk and leiomyoma development. The uterine smooth muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس رحلي) is classified as a benign neoplasm of the uterus (benign neoplasm of uterus) and is conceptually derived from enlarged uterine fibroids (أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة), as indicated by the mapping relationship. Enlarged uterine fibroids themselves fall under the broader category of uterine/endometrial disorders (UTERINE/ENDOMETRIAL DISORDERS). Together, these relationships highlight a network in which hormonal prophylaxis, prior congenital anomalies, gestational measurements, and uterine scar characteristics intersect with both miscarriage risk and the development of benign uterine smooth‑muscle neoplasms, emphasizing the importance of integrated assessment in reproductive and gynecologic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial‑like tissue that is normally located in the uterus, and its ectopic growth can cause pain, inflammation, and fertility issues. Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 730
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is identified as a specific disorder of the cervix (اضطراب في عنق الرحم) and represents a subtype of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility, in turn, is closely linked to intrauterine adhesions (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) and may arise as a consequence of inflammation of the uterine lining (الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ). These interconnections indicate that cervical pathology, uterine lining inflammation, and intrauterine adhesions can all contribute to the development of female infertility, underscoring the importance of evaluating cervical health, endometrial status, and the presence of adhesions when assessing and managing infertility in women.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone are therapeutic agents that may help prevent إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). Clinical observations indicate that certain obstetric factors, such as abnormal SIZE DATES and an unknown type of uterine scar, are associated with an increased risk of إجهاض تلقائي. Recognizing both the potential protective role of these progesterone‑related medications and the risk contributed by specific uterine and gestational characteristics is important for optimizing management strategies aimed at reducing the incidence of spontaneous abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 438
retrieved_docs.content: ureteral obstruction, seizures, lung disorders, bone and joint inflammation, female infertility, endometrial implantation, tongue disorders, mitral valve stenosis, retinal detachment, paralysis, lower‑extremity ulcers and headache including migraine, indicating a birth‑related component across these entities. Osteoarthritis is categorized as a form of bone and joint inflammation (التهاب العظم والمفصل), and female infertility is associated with endometrial implantation, reflecting the known link between infertility and endometriosis. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how therapeutic strategies, preventive measures, developmental biology, microbial factors and congenital status intersect across diverse medical conditions, providing an integrated framework for understanding disease mechanisms and guiding comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1780

Clinical observations indicate that a congenital malformation in a previous pregnancy, abnormal size dates, and an unknown type of uterine scar are each clinically associated with an increased likelihood of إجهاض تلقائي. Recognizing both the potential protective role of specific progestogenic medications and the presence of these obstetric risk factors is essential for optimizing patient counseling, monitoring, and management strategies aimed at reducing the incidence of spontaneous abortion.
